mnemonics.title: Page Replacement Algorithms
mnemonics.content:
FIFO: First In First Out - Oldest page out
LRU: Least Recently Used - Oldest in time
OPT: OPTimal - Future knowledge needed
CLOCK: Circular reference bit check

acronyms.usage: Hardware cache for fast address translation
Page replacement algorithm based on temporal locality
Page replacement based on arrival time
Hardware that translates virtual to physical addresses
Entry in page table containing frame number and control bits
Process of converting virtual address to physical address

visualTips.visualization: Draw two boxes: Logical Address (Page # | Offset) → Page Table → Physical Address (Frame # | Offset). Show how page number indexes into table to get frame number, then combine with offset.
Draw three boxes: Logical Address (Segment # | Offset) → Segment Table (contains base and limit) → Physical Address. Show how offset is checked against limit, then added to base.
Show a flowchart: CPU generates virtual address → TLB searched → If hit, get frame number → If miss, search page table (with page fault check) → Update TLB → Get frame number.
Draw steps in circle: Page Fault → OS checks valid page → Locate on disk → Find free frame → Read page from disk → Update page table → Restart instruction.

analogies.analogy: Think of paging like a book with fixed-size chapters (pages). You can read chapters in any physical order (frames) as long as you have the chapter number (page table) to find where each chapter is physically located.

Virtual memory is like a library's inter-library loan system. You don't need all books in your personal collection (RAM) - the library (OS) can fetch any book you need (page fault) from the central storage (disk) when you request it.

TLB is like a commonly used phone number list kept on your desk (fast access) vs. looking up numbers in a phone book (slower, like page table). Most of the time you find what you need in the quick list.
Thrashing is like constantly shuffling papers on your desk trying to find what you need but never actually reading them - you're so busy organizing that you never get work done.
Segmentation is like organizing a library by sections (Fiction, Science, History) of different sizes rather than uniformly sized shelves. Each section can grow or shrink independently.
